macro_events.event_summary: IEA chief warns of the "biggest energy security threat in history" amid Iran war and fuel shortage concerns, signaling elevated oil supply risk.
primary_drivers: Oil supply disruption risk premium raising crude prices
Geopolitical risk driving energy security/defense spending
Push toward domestic energy production and LNG export capacity

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with substantial US/Guyana/Permian upstream production; crude price spike flows directly to upstream earnings with limited Iran/Strait of Hormuz operational exposure.
US-weighted integrated producer (Permian, Gulf of Mexico, Kazakhstan) benefits from higher realized crude prices; diversified away from direct Iran exposure.
Pure-play upstream E&P with ~100% leverage to crude prices and heavy US shale exposure; highest earnings sensitivity among large-caps to a sustained oil price premium.
Cheniere is the largest US LNG exporter; energy security narrative accelerates European/Asian demand for non-Russian, non-Middle East gas supply, supporting long-term contract pricing.
Permian-concentrated producer with high operating leverage to WTI; every $10 move in crude materially shifts FCF given debt load.
Oilfield services sees activity/pricing power inflect when sustained high crude triggers upstream capex response; North America-weighted revenue mix.
Iran war escalation and energy security framing drives defense budget tailwinds; Lockheed's missile defense (PAC-3, THAAD) directly relevant to Middle East force posture.
Texas Pacific Land — pure Permian royalty/surface owner with zero opex leverage to crude; quintessential oil-price call option with no operational Middle East risk.

losers.rationale: Delta has historically lower fuel hedging than peers; jet fuel is ~25-30% of operating costs, and sustained crude spike compresses margins directly.
United runs long-haul international routes (including Middle East/India) — double hit from fuel costs and potential airspace closures/rerouting around Iran.
Southwest is 100% jet fuel exposed with recently expired favorable hedges; domestic pricing power limited to pass through fuel cost spike.
Cruise operator with heavy bunker fuel costs and discretionary travel demand that weakens on geopolitical anxiety; Mediterranean/Middle East itinerary disruption risk.
Commodity chemicals producer with naphtha/natural gas liquids feedstock exposure; margin compression when oil-linked input costs outrun end-product pricing.
Energy cost shock acts as consumer tax on low-end shopper discretionary spend; diesel/logistics cost inflation pressures already-thin retail margins.

headlines: 'We are facing the biggest energy security threat in history,' IEA chief tells CNBC

macro_events.event_summary: Former Canadian PM Trudeau claims U.S. and European economic coercion (tariffs/trade friction) nearly pushed Canada to deepen ties with China — a rhetorical/political commentary piece rather than a new policy action.
primary_drivers: Political commentary without new policy — no change in tariff schedules, trade flows, or capital controls
Retrospective framing of existing US-Canada trade tensions already priced in
headlines: Economic coercion from U.S. and Europe almost drove Canada 'into China's arms,' says Trudeau
